back: Serverless, interactive query service that runs standard SQL directly against data in S3 (via Presto/Trino) without needing to load it anywhere. Pay per query based on data scanned (per TB), not per hour, so partitioning data and using columnar formats like Parquet/ORC in S3 dramatically cuts cost. Common exam pattern: ad hoc/one-off analytics on data lake data with no need to manage infrastructure or provision clusters, unlike Redshift or EMR.

Marketplace for finding, subscribing to, and receiving third-party data directly into S3, Redshift, or via API, with billing handled through AWS. Exam angle: use it when a scenario needs external/third-party datasets (e.g. financial, weather, demographic) delivered and kept up to date without building your own ingestion pipeline from a vendor.

Orchestration service for scheduling and moving/transforming data between AWS compute/storage services and on-premises sources on a recurring schedule. Largely legacy/deprecated in favor of AWS Glue and Step Functions — on the exam, if a question involves ETL orchestration, Glue or Step Functions is almost always the better/expected answer.

Managed Hadoop/Spark cluster service for big-data processing (Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Presto, HBase), deployable on EC2, EKS, EMR Serverless, or Outposts. Spot Instances on task nodes are a common cost-optimization choice, since losing a task node doesn't lose data — only core/master nodes hold HDFS data.
Serverless ETL and data-catalog service. Glue Crawlers infer schema and populate the Glue Data Catalog; Glue Jobs (Spark or Python shell) run the actual data transform.

Runs SQL or Apache Flink applications directly against streaming data from Kinesis Data Streams or Firehose in real time, without needing a separate consumer application. Used for real-time aggregations, anomaly detection, or windowed computations on a stream — distinguishes from Athena, which queries data at rest, not in motion.

Fully managed delivery service that loads streaming data into S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, or Splunk, with optional Lambda-based transformation and format conversion (e.g. JSON to Parquet) in-flight. Near-real-time — buffers by size/time, typically seconds to minutes, not true real-time.

Real-time streaming ingestion service partitioned into shards; each shard supports 1MB/sec or 1,000 records/sec write and 2MB/sec read. Data is retained 24 hours by default (extendable up to 365 days) and can be replayed by multiple independent consumers.

Centralizes and simplifies setting up a secure data lake on S3, layering fine-grained (row/column/tag-based) access control on top of the Glue Data Catalog. Exam angle: it's the answer whenever a scenario needs centralized permission management across many users/accounts querying the same S3-based data lake via Athena, Redshift Spectrum, or EMR, rather than managing individual S3 bucket policies.

Managed Apache Kafka — AWS runs the brokers and Zookeeper/KRaft control plane, but you still manage topics, partitions, and consumer groups yourself (unlike the fully serverless Kinesis). Choose MSK over Kinesis Data Streams when a scenario explicitly requires open-source Kafka compatibility, existing Kafka client code, or portability across clouds; MSK Serverless removes capacity planning if you want Kafka semantics without managing brokers.

Managed cluster for the OpenSearch/Elasticsearch engine, used for log analytics, full-text search, and real-time application monitoring (often paired with CloudWatch Logs or Kinesis Data Firehose as a sink). Exam distinction from Athena/Redshift: OpenSearch is optimized for full-text search and near-real-time log/metric dashboards, not SQL-style structured analytics.

Serverless BI/dashboarding tool with pay-per-session pricing and SPICE, an in-memory columnar cache that speeds up dashboards without hitting the source data store on every view. Exam angle: it's the visualization/reporting layer on top of Athena, Redshift, RDS, or S3 — not a data processing or storage service itself.

No-code, fully managed integration service for bidirectional data transfer between SaaS apps (Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, Google Analytics, Slack, etc.) and AWS services like S3 and Redshift, with built-in field mapping, filtering, and validation. Exam trigger phrase: 'transfer data to/from a SaaS application without writing code' — that's AppFlow, not Glue or Lambda.

Managed GraphQL (and Pub/Sub-over-WebSocket) API service with built-in resolvers to DynamoDB, Lambda, RDS, OpenSearch, and HTTP endpoints, plus real-time subscriptions and offline data sync for mobile apps.

Serverless event bus for routing events between AWS services, SaaS partners, and custom applications using rule-based pattern matching, with schema registry and archive/replay support. Also underlies scheduled/cron-based triggers (replacing CloudWatch Events).

Managed message broker for ActiveMQ and RabbitMQ, aimed at applications migrating from on-premises brokers that already speak JMS, NMS, AMQP, MQTT, or STOMP protocols. Exam trigger: 'lift-and-shift a legacy application using an existing message broker protocol' points to MQ, not SQS/SNS, which are AWS-proprietary APIs requiring code changes.

Pub/sub messaging: publishers send one message to a topic and it fans out to all subscribers (SQS queues, Lambda, HTTP/S endpoints, email, SMS, mobile push) simultaneously. Exam pattern: use the 'fan-out' architecture (SNS topic -> multiple SQS queues) when one event needs to trigger several independent, decoupled downstream processes.
Managed message queue for decoupling producers and consumers. Visibility timeout (default 30s) and dead-letter queues (for handling repeatedly-failing messages) are frequent exam scenario details.

Serverless workflow orchestrator using state machines (Amazon States Language) to coordinate Lambda, ECS, SNS, SQS, and other AWS services with built-in retry, error handling, parallel, and wait states. Exam distinction: Step Functions is for orchestrating multi-step, long-running, or conditional workflows across services — where Lambda alone would need brittle custom state-tracking and retry logic.

Fully managed service for creating and managing scalable blockchain networks using Hyperledger Fabric or Ethereum, removing the operational burden of provisioning nodes and managing certificates. A narrow-scope service on the exam — mainly recognized as the answer to 'need a managed blockchain network,' rarely appears in complex scenario questions.

Deprecated/discontinued service that let organizations deploy Alexa devices for shared spaces (conference rooms) and skills for employees. Very unlikely to appear meaningfully on a current SAP-C02 exam — recognize the name but don't invest study time here.
Managed outbound (and inbound) email-sending service with high deliverability, DKIM/SPF/DMARC support, and event publishing (bounces, complaints, deliveries) to SNS or Kinesis for tracking.

Proactive cost/usage alerting: define a budget threshold (cost, usage, RI/Savings Plan utilization/coverage) and trigger SNS notifications or even automated actions (e.g. applying an IAM policy or stopping instances) when thresholds are crossed.

The most granular, comprehensive billing data AWS produces — line-item detail for every resource, usage type, and API operation, delivered to an S3 bucket (often queried via Athena or loaded into Redshift/QuickSight). Exam trigger: 'need the most detailed possible cost data for custom analysis' points to CUR, not Cost Explorer, which is a curated UI over similar but less granular data.

Built-in UI/API for visualizing and analyzing historical cost and usage trends, with forecasting and RI/Savings Plan recommendations. Good for interactive investigation of cost drivers and anomalies; for automated alerting use Budgets, and for raw exportable data use CUR.

Commitment-based discount (up to ~72%) in exchange for a $/hour spend commitment over 1 or 3 years. Compute Savings Plans apply automatically across EC2 (any instance family/region/OS), Fargate, and Lambda; EC2 Instance Savings Plans give a deeper discount but lock to a specific instance family in a region.

Fully managed service to deploy a web app or API directly from source code or a container image, with automatic build, deploy, load balancing, and scaling — no VPC/cluster/orchestration setup required. Exam distinction from Elastic Beanstalk/ECS: App Runner is the simplest, most opinionated option, ideal for a single containerized web service without needing fine-grained infrastructure control.

Fully managed batch job scheduler that dynamically provisions the optimal compute (EC2, Spot, or Fargate) to run queued containerized jobs, handling retries and dependency ordering. Exam trigger: 'run large numbers of batch computing jobs (simulations, ML training, nightly processing) without managing servers' — Batch handles queuing/scheduling that you'd otherwise have to build yourself on top of raw ECS/EC2.
Core IaaS compute, with the broadest range of instance families/processors (Intel, AMD, Arm) and purchase models to match workload cost, performance, and availability needs.

Automatically adds/removes EC2 instances based on scaling policies: target tracking (maintain a metric like CPU at X%), step scaling (react to CloudWatch alarm breaches), scheduled scaling (known traffic patterns), and predictive scaling (ML-based forecasting). Also handles fleet health — replacing unhealthy instances automatically. Exam nuance: combining an ASG across multiple AZs (and using warm pools to pre-initialize instances) is a common high-availability + fast-scaling pattern.

PaaS: upload code and Beanstalk provisions and manages the underlying EC2, ASG, ELB, and RDS resources, while still giving you access to the underlying resources if needed (unlike Lambda/App Runner which fully abstract them). Exam distinction: Beanstalk is for teams who want managed infrastructure but still need some control/visibility into servers — a middle ground between raw EC2 and fully serverless options.
Serverless compute engine for containers — works with both ECS and EKS, removing the need to provision, patch, or scale EC2 worker nodes. You pay per vCPU/memory reserved by the task/pod.

Event-driven serverless compute; max execution timeout is 15 minutes, memory configurable 128MB–10,240MB (with proportional vCPU), and ephemeral /tmp storage up to 10GB. Cold starts and concurrency limits (default account concurrency limit, reserved/provisioned concurrency to control it) are frequent exam topics.

Simplified VPS offering with bundled compute, storage, and networking at flat monthly pricing — aimed at simple websites/apps and users who don't need the full flexibility (or complexity) of EC2/VPC. Exam signal: 'simplest, cheapest option for a small, predictable workload with minimal AWS expertise needed' points to Lightsail rather than EC2.

Extends native AWS infrastructure (compute, storage, select services, and control plane) into an on-premises data center, fully managed by AWS on customer-owned or Outposts-form-factor hardware. Exam trigger: low-latency access to on-prem systems, local data processing/residency requirements, or migrating apps with on-prem interdependencies that can't yet move to the cloud — distinct from Local Zones (AWS-owned infrastructure closer to users) and Wavelength (telco 5G edge).

Managed MQTT/HTTPS/LoRaWAN message broker enabling secure bidirectional communication between IoT devices and the cloud, plus a device shadow (last-known-state) service and a rules engine to route messages to other AWS services. Exam-relevant if a question needs device connectivity at scale with per-device authentication (X.509 certs).

Managed, private Docker/OCI container image registry integrated with IAM for access control, with vulnerability scanning built in. It's the registry component only — pairs with ECS/EKS/Fargate for actually running containers.

AWS-native container orchestrator (simpler than Kubernetes) supporting two launch types: EC2 (you manage the cluster instances) or Fargate (serverless). Task definitions describe containers, and services keep a desired count of tasks running behind a load balancer.

Extends ECS control plane to manage container workloads running on your own on-premises servers or VMs (external instances) alongside cloud-based ECS tasks, using the standard ECS APIs. Niche but exam-testable for hybrid container management scenarios that need a single ECS control plane spanning on-prem and cloud.

Fully managed Kubernetes control plane (etcd, API server managed by AWS) with worker nodes on EC2, Fargate, or Outposts/on-prem via EKS Anywhere/Distro. Choose over ECS when the workload requires Kubernetes-native APIs, Helm charts, existing K8s manifests, or portability across cloud providers — a very common SAP-C02 discriminator question.

Deployment option to run EKS's Kubernetes distribution entirely on your own on-premises infrastructure (bare metal, VMware), independent of an AWS Region connection — for air-gapped or fully on-prem Kubernetes needs while still using the same tooling as cloud EKS.

The open-source Kubernetes distribution that EKS itself is built on; you can download and run it yourself, decoupled from the managed EKS service, if you want AWS's tested/patched Kubernetes builds without using AWS's control plane.

MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible relational engine with storage auto-scaling up to 128TB, 6 copies of data across 3 AZs by default, and up to 15 low-latency read replicas (vs. 5 for standard RDS) with typically single-digit-ms replica lag.

On-demand autoscaling Aurora configuration that scales capacity (in Aurora Capacity Units) up/down — including to near-zero for v2 with minimal ACUs — based on load, without managing instances. Exam trigger: intermittent, unpredictable, or spiky database workloads (e.g. dev/test, low-traffic apps) where paying for constantly-provisioned capacity would be wasteful.

MongoDB-compatible (API-level, not engine-level) managed document database, storage auto-scales up to 128TB similar to Aurora's architecture. Exam distinction from DynamoDB: DocumentDB targets teams with existing MongoDB workloads/drivers wanting compatibility, whereas DynamoDB is AWS's own proprietary NoSQL key-value/document store with different scaling and consistency model.

Serverless key-value/document NoSQL database with single-digit-millisecond latency at any scale. DAX adds an in-memory cache layer for microsecond reads; Global Tables give active-active multi-Region replication; Streams enable event-driven processing on data changes.
Managed in-memory cache (Redis OSS/Valkey or Memcached) sitting in front of a database to reduce read latency/load.

Serverless, Apache Cassandra-compatible (CQL API) wide-column database. Exam trigger: 'existing Cassandra workload/skills, need a managed, serverless equivalent' — a narrow but recognizable exam signal distinct from DynamoDB's proprietary API.

Fully managed graph database supporting property graph (Gremlin, openCypher) and RDF (SPARQL) models, optimized for highly connected data. Exam trigger: relationship-heavy queries like fraud detection, social networks, or recommendation engines where traversing connections matters more than tabular joins.
Managed relational database supporting 6 engines (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Aurora).

Petabyte-scale columnar data warehouse for complex analytical (OLAP) SQL queries across structured/semi-structured data. Redshift Spectrum lets you query data directly in S3 without loading it in, and RA3 node types decouple compute/storage scaling.

Purpose-built, serverless time-series database that automatically tiers data from an in-memory store (recent, frequently-queried data) to cost-optimized magnetic storage (historical data), with built-in time-series functions (interpolation, smoothing). Exam trigger: IoT sensor data, application/infrastructure metrics, or any workload defined by 'store and analyze data points ordered by time at massive scale.'

Browser-based cloud IDE with a built-in terminal that has direct AWS CLI access; underlying compute typically runs on an EC2 instance you own. Mostly a developer-convenience/collaboration tool — low weight on the SAP-C02 exam beyond simple recognition.

Managed artifact repository for language-native package managers (npm, Maven, pip, NuGet, etc.), letting teams host private packages and proxy public repositories (e.g. npmjs, PyPI) through a single controlled endpoint.

Fully managed build service: compiles source, runs tests, and produces deployable artifacts, scaling build capacity automatically and billing per build-minute. It's the 'build' stage that plugs into CodePipeline, and a frequent exam pattern is running CodeBuild inside a VPC to reach private resources (e.g. an RDS instance) during integration tests.

Managed private Git repository hosting on AWS, integrated with IAM for access control. Note: AWS has been deprioritizing CodeCommit for new customers in favor of third-party Git providers (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) integrated via CodeStar Connections/CodePipeline — exam questions may still reference it, but recognize third-party Git integration as the more current pattern.

Automates code deployments to EC2, on-premises servers, Lambda, or ECS, supporting in-place and blue/green deployment strategies with automatic rollback on failed health checks/alarms. Exam-critical: CodeDeploy is specifically the 'deploy' stage — for Lambda/ECS it enables canary and linear traffic-shifting deployments, a common exam scenario for minimizing blast radius of a bad release.

Orchestrates the end-to-end CI/CD release process (source → build → test → deploy) by chaining other services (CodeCommit/GitHub, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CloudFormation, etc.) into automated stages triggered on every code change. Think of it as the 'glue'/orchestrator connecting the individual Code* services and third-party tools into one pipeline.

Deprecated unified dashboard that used to bundle project templates and provisioning for CodeCommit/CodeBuild/CodeDeploy/CodePipeline into one console view. AWS is discontinuing CodeStar — for the current exam, expect CodePipeline/CodeBuild/CodeDeploy to be referenced individually rather than via CodeStar.

Distributed tracing service that visualizes request paths across microservices (a 'service map'), pinpointing latency bottlenecks and errors across Lambda, API Gateway, ECS, EC2, etc. Exam trigger: 'identify which specific service in a distributed/microservices call chain is causing latency or errors' — X-Ray traces individual requests end-to-end, which CloudWatch metrics alone can't do.

Streams individual desktop applications (not a full desktop) to a browser, fully managed, with automatic scaling of the underlying fleet. Exam distinction from WorkSpaces: AppStream 2.0 streams specific applications to users (including external/non-employee users), while WorkSpaces provisions an entire persistent virtual desktop per user.

Managed persistent virtual desktop (VDI) service — each user gets a full desktop (Windows or Linux), unlike AppStream 2.0's single-application streaming. Exam trigger: 'provide employees with a full, persistent desktop environment accessible from anywhere' points to WorkSpaces.

Toolchain/framework for building and hosting full-stack web/mobile apps quickly, bundling auth (via Cognito), API (via AppSync/API Gateway), storage (S3), and CI/CD-based hosting into a guided developer workflow. Mostly a frontend/mobile developer productivity tool — light exam weight beyond recognizing what it bundles.

Managed API front door supporting REST, HTTP (cheaper, lower-latency, fewer features), and WebSocket APIs, with built-in throttling, authorization (IAM, Cognito, Lambda authorizers), caching, and request/response transformation.

Runs automated tests for web/mobile apps against real physical devices and desktop browsers in the cloud, without provisioning test infrastructure yourself. Niche, low-weight exam topic — recognize it as the answer to 'test my app across many real mobile devices/browsers.'
Multi-channel marketing communication/campaign engine (email, SMS, push, voice) with audience segmentation and analytics on engagement.

Filters, transforms, and enriches raw IoT message data before storing it in a time-series-optimized store for later analysis/querying (e.g. via SQL). Sits downstream of IoT Core in a typical IoT data pipeline.

Continuously audits IoT device fleet configurations against security best practices and detects abnormal device behavior (e.g. unusual traffic patterns suggesting compromise), then can trigger mitigation actions.

Onboards, organizes, monitors, and remotely manages IoT device fleets at scale, including over-the-air (OTA) firmware/software updates. Distinguish from IoT Device Defender (security monitoring) — Device Management is operational fleet management.

Detects and responds to events/patterns across many IoT devices or sensors (e.g. equipment failure, changes in operating state) and triggers downstream actions, using a managed detector-model concept rather than requiring you to write custom stream-processing logic.

Extends AWS to edge devices — lets devices run local Lambda functions, ML inference, and messaging even when disconnected from the cloud, syncing back when connectivity resumes. Exam trigger: 'IoT devices need to keep functioning and making decisions during intermittent/no internet connectivity' points to Greengrass.

Purpose-built for collecting, organizing, and computing metrics from industrial equipment (factories, machinery) at scale, including standard industrial performance calculations, to support predictive maintenance and operational dashboards.

Simplest IoT integration — lets simple, pre-configured devices (like an AWS IoT button) trigger a single Lambda function with one action, with no custom firmware/device management needed. Very narrow use case, low exam weight.

NLP service that extracts entities, key phrases, sentiment, and language from unstructured text, plus a Medical variant (Comprehend Medical) for clinical text. Exam trigger: 'extract insights/sentiment from unstructured text documents' without building/training your own NLP model.

Fully managed service that builds time-series forecasting models (e.g. demand/inventory forecasting) using ML, without requiring ML expertise — takes historical + related time-series data and produces forecasts automatically.

Managed ML service specifically for detecting potentially fraudulent online activity (e.g. payment fraud, fake account creation) using pre-built fraud-detection model templates, without needing to build custom ML pipelines.

Enterprise search service powered by ML/NLP that returns precise answers (not just linked documents) from unstructured content across multiple connected data sources (S3, SharePoint, Confluence, etc.) — think 'intelligent enterprise search,' distinct from OpenSearch's more general-purpose search/log analytics role.

Builds conversational chatbot interfaces (voice and text) using the same NLU engine as Alexa — handles intent recognition and slot-filling; commonly paired with Lambda for business logic and Connect for contact-center voice bots.

Managed recommendation-engine service (the same tech behind Amazon.com's recommendations) — feed it user/item interaction data and it returns real-time personalized recommendations, without requiring you to build/train a recommender model from scratch.

Text-to-speech service converting text into natural-sounding speech audio, including Neural TTS voices and SSML control over pronunciation/prosody. Pairs with Transcribe (the inverse: speech-to-text) in many exam scenarios about accessibility or IVR systems.
Pre-trained computer-vision service for image/video analysis — object/scene detection, facial analysis/comparison, and content moderation — without needing ML expertise or custom model training.

Full ML platform to build, train, and deploy custom machine-learning models — covers notebook instances (Jupyter), managed training jobs (including distributed/Spot training), and managed inference endpoints (real-time, serverless, or batch/async).

ML service that extracts text, tables, forms, and key-value pairs from scanned documents — goes beyond plain OCR by understanding document structure (e.g. extracting a specific form field), a key exam distinguisher from simple OCR.

Speech-to-text service converting audio into text, supporting features like speaker identification, custom vocabulary, and PII redaction. The inverse of Polly (text-to-speech); frequently paired in call-center analytics or captioning scenarios.
Neural machine translation service for translating text between languages in real time or batch, without needing custom translation models.

Unified command-line tool for scripting/automating interactions with any AWS service via its API — the scripting counterpart to the Management Console; exam relevance is mostly around knowing it's the tool for automation/scripting rather than any specific feature.

Infrastructure-as-Code service: define AWS resources in declarative JSON/YAML templates, then create/update/delete them as a single managed 'stack' with automatic rollback on failure. StackSets extend this to deploy the same template across multiple accounts/regions — a key exam pattern for consistent multi-account provisioning (often alongside AWS Organizations/Control Tower).

Records API-level activity (who did what, when, from where) across the AWS account/organization for auditing, governance, and security investigation — every console, CLI, and SDK action shows up as an event here.

Central metrics, alarms, and dashboards service — collects metrics from AWS resources (and custom metrics), triggers alarms (e.g. to feed Auto Scaling or SNS), and visualizes operational health. The core building block for automated reactive scaling and operational monitoring across the exam.

Centralized log aggregation from EC2, Lambda, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and more, with search/filter, metric filters (turn log patterns into CloudWatch metrics/alarms), and subscription filters (stream logs in real time to Lambda, Kinesis, or OpenSearch) — the log-specific half of CloudWatch, distinct from the metrics/alarms half.

Analyzes historical utilization (CPU, memory, network via CloudWatch Agent) of EC2, Auto Scaling groups, EBS, Lambda, and ECS on Fargate, then recommends more cost/performance-optimal resource types/sizes — a rightsizing recommendation engine, distinct from Trusted Advisor's broader best-practice checks.

Continuously records AWS resource configurations and evaluates them against rules (managed or custom) for compliance, keeping a full configuration history/timeline per resource. Exam-critical: Config answers 'is this resource compliant with our policy, and what did its configuration look like at any point in time' — paired with CloudTrail (who made the API call) and often triggering remediation via Systems Manager Automation or Lambda.

Automates setting up a secure, well-architected multi-account AWS environment (a 'landing zone') on top of AWS Organizations, applying preventive (SCP-based) and detective (Config-based) guardrails automatically. Exam trigger: 'quickly and consistently set up governance across many new/existing AWS accounts' — Control Tower orchestrates Organizations + Config + SSO/IAM Identity Center together rather than being a standalone primitive.

Personalized view of AWS service health events and scheduled changes that specifically affect your account's resources (vs. the public Service Health Dashboard, which shows general regional/service status) — useful for proactive operational awareness of AWS-side issues impacting you.

Tracks and enforces software licensing rules (e.g. Microsoft, Oracle, SAP license terms) across EC2 and on-premises usage to avoid over-deployment/non-compliance — relevant to BYOL (bring-your-own-license) migration scenarios.

Managed Grafana for visualizing metrics/logs/traces from multiple sources (including CloudWatch and Prometheus) — chosen specifically when an organization already has Grafana dashboards/expertise and wants that experience without self-hosting Grafana.

Serverless, Prometheus-compatible metrics store for container workloads (commonly EKS/ECS), letting teams keep using PromQL and existing Prometheus-based tooling without operating Prometheus servers themselves — pairs naturally with Managed Grafana for visualization.
The web UI for AWS — mentioned on the exam mainly in contrast to CLI/SDK/CloudFormation as the manual, non-programmatic way of interacting with AWS.
Embeds AWS compute/storage at the edge of telecom providers' 5G networks, giving ultra-low-latency access for mobile/connected devices (e.g. AR/VR, real-time gaming, connected vehicles).

ML-powered code review (CodeGuru Reviewer) and application performance profiling (CodeGuru Profiler) — automatically flags bugs, security issues, and inefficient code patterns during development/CI, and identifies CPU/latency hotspots in running applications.

Central management of multiple AWS accounts — consolidated billing, Service Control Policies (SCPs) for guardrails (preventive controls that even the account root user can't bypass), and organizational units (OUs) for grouping accounts. Foundational to almost every multi-account exam scenario; SCPs restrict what IAM policies can ever grant, they don't grant permissions themselves.

Platform-engineering tool letting a central team define standardized infrastructure/deployment templates for containerized and serverless apps, which developers then self-service deploy from — aimed at large orgs wanting to enforce consistent architecture patterns without developers writing their own IaC.

Lets administrators curate a catalog of pre-approved CloudFormation-based products (VMs, databases, full stacks) that end users/teams can self-service launch, ensuring compliance/standardization without giving users broad provisioning permissions. Exam trigger: 'let developers self-provision approved resources without direct IAM permissions to create them.'

Central place to view and request increases for AWS service limits (e.g. EC2 instance limits, VPC counts) across services, and to set CloudWatch alarms when usage approaches a quota — relevant whenever a scenario mentions hitting an AWS default limit.

Umbrella operations toolset: Session Manager (shell access without SSH keys/bastion hosts/open inbound ports — a common exam answer for secure EC2 access), Patch Manager (automated patching), Run Command (ad hoc remote commands at scale), Parameter Store (config/secrets storage), and State Manager (enforce desired configuration state) — a frequent source of 'operate/secure a fleet of instances without X' exam answers.

Automated best-practice checks across cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, service limits, and operational excellence, with the depth of checks gated by support plan (Business/Enterprise support unlocks the full check set).

Free, self-service tool to review a workload against the six pillars of the Well-Architected Framework (operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, sustainability), producing a report of risks and improvement suggestions — a structured, workload-level architectural review, distinct from Trusted Advisor's account-wide automated checks.

Cloud media transcoding service converting video/audio into different formats/bitrates for playback across devices. Being discontinued by AWS (Nov 13, 2025) — on a current exam, prefer/expect AWS Elemental MediaConvert as the modern media transcoding answer instead.

Securely ingests, stores, and indexes streaming video from many devices for playback, ML analysis (e.g. via Rekognition Video), or custom processing — the video-specific counterpart to Kinesis Data Streams, purpose-built for time-indexed media rather than generic records.

Collects usage/configuration/dependency data from on-premises servers (agentless or agent-based) to inform migration planning — feeds data into Migration Hub so you know what you're migrating and how systems depend on each other before you move them.

AWS's recommended lift-and-shift (rehost) tool — continuously replicates on-premises or other-cloud servers to AWS, allowing low-downtime cutover; it has effectively replaced the legacy Server Migration Service (SMS) as the current exam-relevant rehosting tool.

Migrates databases to AWS (homogeneous or heterogeneous, e.g. Oracle to Aurora) with minimal downtime via continuous replication (CDC). For heterogeneous migrations (different source/target engines), DMS is almost always paired with the Schema Conversion Tool (SCT), which converts the schema/code first — DMS alone only migrates data, not schema logic like stored procedures.

Automates and accelerates large-scale, one-time or recurring data transfer between on-premises storage (NFS/SMB) and S3/EFS/FSx, or between AWS storage services, with built-in encryption, scheduling, and validation — much faster than manual scripted transfers over the same links.

Single console to track the progress of migrations happening across multiple AWS and partner migration tools (Application Discovery Service, DMS, MGN, etc.) — a tracking/visibility layer, not a migration engine itself.

Converts database schema and code (stored procedures, views, functions) from one engine to another (e.g. Oracle to PostgreSQL) ahead of a heterogeneous migration. Always paired with DMS in exam scenarios: SCT converts the schema/code, DMS migrates the actual data.

Physical devices for offline bulk data transfer and edge computing: Snowcone (smallest, portable, includes some compute), Snowball Edge (larger, storage- or compute-optimized, can run EC2/Lambda at the edge), and Snowmobile (exabyte-scale, a literal shipping container on a truck, now largely superseded by high-count Snowball Edge fleets).

Fully managed SFTP/FTPS/FTP endpoints backed directly by S3 or EFS, letting partners/legacy systems keep using familiar file-transfer protocols while data lands natively in AWS storage — avoids needing to stand up and maintain your own FTP servers.

AWS's CDN — caches content at edge locations close to users to reduce latency and origin load, integrates with Shield/WAF for edge-level DDoS/application protection, and supports both static (S3) and dynamic (ALB/EC2/custom) origins, plus Lambda@Edge/CloudFront Functions for edge-side logic. Frequently the front door in exam architectures needing global low-latency delivery plus security.
Dedicated, private physical network connection from on-premises to AWS, bypassing the public internet for consistent latency/bandwidth and reduced data transfer cost at scale.
AWS's managed load balancing service, automatically distributing incoming application traffic across multiple targets and scaling with demand.

Routes user traffic over AWS's private global backbone network to the optimal (closest/healthiest) regional endpoint using static anycast IPs, improving performance and providing fast failover between regions. Exam distinction from CloudFront: Global Accelerator improves routing/failover for any TCP/UDP traffic (not just HTTP-cacheable content) and is often chosen for non-HTTP protocols or when static IPs are required, whereas CloudFront is specifically for caching HTTP(S) content at the edge.

Provides private connectivity from your VPC to supported AWS services, partner services, or your own services in another VPC, without traffic ever traversing the public internet or requiring VPC peering/route table changes. Exam trigger: 'expose a service to another VPC/account without peering and without exposing it to the internet' — PrivateLink (via an interface endpoint) is the answer, distinct from VPC Peering, which fully connects two VPCs' networks.

Managed DNS with health checks and multiple routing policies: simple, weighted (percentage-based traffic split), latency-based (route to lowest-latency region), failover (active-passive DR), and geolocation/geoproximity (route by user location). Choosing the correct routing policy for a given availability/performance/compliance requirement is a recurring exam pattern.

Acts as a centralized network router: each VPC needs only one Transit Gateway attachment (regardless of how many other VPCs/networks it must reach), a Direct Connect Gateway attaches to the Transit Gateway to bring on-premises connectivity in through one centralized connection, and AWS RAM shares the Transit Gateway across accounts so new accounts/VPCs can be added without building a web of pairwise connections.

Your isolated virtual network — subnets (public/private, tied to an AZ), route tables, internet/NAT gateways, security groups (stateful, instance-level) and NACLs (stateless, subnet-level) are the core building blocks. Exam-critical distinction: security groups only allow rules (implicit deny) and are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed); NACLs support explicit allow AND deny rules and are stateless (return traffic must be explicitly allowed too).

Encrypted IPsec tunnel(s) over the public internet connecting on-premises networks to a VPC (via a Virtual Private Gateway or Transit Gateway) — fast to provision (minutes) but subject to public internet variability, unlike Direct Connect's dedicated private line. Commonly paired with Direct Connect as an encrypted backup path, or used alone for lower-cost/lower-urgency hybrid connectivity.

Self-service portal for downloading AWS compliance reports (SOC, PCI, ISO, etc.) and accepting agreements (like a BAA for HIPAA workloads) — purely a compliance-documentation repository, not a security control itself.

Continuously collects evidence of resource configurations/activity against prebuilt or custom compliance frameworks (e.g. PCI DSS, HIPAA, GDPR) to simplify audit preparation — automates the evidence-gathering that Config/CloudTrail data would otherwise require manually assembling.

Provisions, manages, and auto-renews public and private SSL/TLS certificates for use with ELB, CloudFront, and API Gateway — ACM-issued public certificates are free but can only be used with these integrated AWS services, not exported for use on arbitrary servers (that requires ACM Private CA or importing your own cert).

Dedicated, single-tenant, FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware security module giving you exclusive control over cryptographic keys — unlike KMS, AWS has zero access to your key material. Exam trigger: strict compliance/regulatory requirements mandating a dedicated HSM or direct control over key storage hardware, where KMS's shared/managed model isn't sufficient.
User authentication/authorization for web/mobile apps, via User Pools and Identity Pools.
Automatically collects and correlates log data (VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, GuardDuty findings) into a visual, interactive graph to speed up investigating the root cause of a security finding.

Managed Microsoft Active Directory (or a lighter-weight Simple AD / AD Connector to proxy an existing on-prem AD) for applications and workloads needing native AD/LDAP integration — commonly needed for lift-and-shift Windows workloads or FSx for Windows File Server authentication.

Centrally manages AWS WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, Security Groups, and Network Firewall policies across all accounts/resources in an AWS Organization from one place — ensures new resources automatically inherit required security policies. Exam trigger: 'enforce consistent WAF/Shield/security group rules across a multi-account organization.'

ML/threat-intelligence-based continuous threat detection analyzing CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, and (with extensions) EKS audit logs and S3 data events, to flag things like compromised credentials, crypto-mining, or reconnaissance activity — a detective control that requires no agents to deploy.

Centralized SSO across multiple AWS accounts (via Organizations) and SAML-enabled third-party apps, using permission sets rather than per-account IAM users — the current recommended way to manage human access to multiple accounts, superseding the older standalone 'AWS SSO' branding.
Controls who (users, groups, roles) can do what (permissions via policies) on which resources within a single account.

Automated vulnerability scanning for EC2 instances, container images (in ECR), and Lambda functions — continuously scans for known CVEs and unintended network exposure, replacing the need for manual/periodic vulnerability assessment scripts.

Managed creation and control of cryptographic keys (envelope encryption under the hood) used to encrypt data across nearly all AWS services (S3, EBS, RDS, etc.), with detailed key-usage auditing via CloudTrail.

ML-powered service that scans S3 buckets to automatically discover, classify, and alert on sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial data) — exam trigger: 'discover where sensitive data lives across many S3 buckets' without manually writing detection rules.

Managed, stateful network-layer firewall for a VPC, supporting fine-grained rules (including intrusion detection/prevention, domain filtering) beyond what Security Groups/NACLs can express — used when a scenario needs deep packet inspection or centralized traffic filtering across many VPCs (often deployed via Transit Gateway/Gateway Load Balancer for centralized inspection architectures).

Shares AWS resources (subnets, Transit Gateways, License Manager configurations, Route 53 Resolver rules, etc.) across accounts within an Organization without duplicating the resource — exam trigger: 'share a subnet/Transit Gateway attachment/etc. across multiple accounts' without creating separate copies of the resource in each account.
Stores, encrypts, and — critically — automatically rotates secrets like database credentials on a schedule, with native RDS/Aurora rotation integration.

Aggregates and normalizes security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and third-party tools into one dashboard, and runs automated compliance checks against standards (CIS, PCI DSS, AWS Foundational Security Best Practices) — the central 'single pane of glass' for security posture across accounts, distinct from any single detection service.

Issues short-lived, temporary security credentials — underpins IAM role assumption (AssumeRole), cross-account access, identity federation, and Cognito Identity Pools. Every 'temporary credentials' or 'cross-account role assumption' exam scenario is ultimately STS under the hood.
DDoS protection: Shield Standard is automatic and free for all customers, covering network/transport layer (L3/L4) attacks.
Application-layer (L7) web firewall with customizable rules (SQL injection, XSS, rate-based rules, geo-blocking) attached to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, or AppSync.

Centralizes and automates backup policies (schedule, retention, cross-region/cross-account copy) across EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, and more from one place, instead of configuring native backup features per-service separately — the answer whenever a scenario wants one unified backup policy/compliance story across many resource types.

Network-attached block storage for EC2, tied to a single AZ (must snapshot to move across AZs — snapshots are stored in S3 behind the scenes). Volume types matter on the exam: gp3 (general purpose baseline), io2/io2 Block Express (highest IOPS, for latency-sensitive/critical DBs), st1 (throughput-optimized HDD for big sequential workloads like big data), sc1 (cold HDD, cheapest, infrequent access).

Continuously replicates on-premises or cloud servers to a low-cost staging area in AWS, allowing fast (minutes) failover/recovery during a disaster — AWS's current recommended DR tool, effectively superseding the older CloudEndure Disaster Recovery branding.

Managed, fully elastic NFS file system that many EC2 instances (and Lambda functions) can mount concurrently across multiple AZs — unlike EBS, which attaches to only one instance at a time (except io2 Multi-Attach in narrow cases). Exam trigger: 'shared file storage accessible by multiple Linux instances simultaneously' points to EFS, not EBS.

Family of managed third-party file systems: FSx for Windows File Server (SMB, AD-integrated, for Windows workloads), FSx for Lustre (high-performance parallel file system for HPC/ML, can link directly to S3 as a data repository), FSx for NetApp ONTAP, and FSx for OpenZFS. Exam trigger: pick the specific FSx variant matching the workload's native file system requirement (e.g. Lustre for HPC/ML, Windows File Server for AD-joined Windows apps).

Object storage with 11 nines of durability, unlimited scale. Storage classes trade cost for retrieval speed/frequency: Standard (frequent access), Standard-IA/One Zone-IA (infrequent access, cheaper storage + retrieval fee), Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves objects between tiers based on access patterns — no retrieval fee, ideal for unpredictable access), and the Glacier family for archival. Lifecycle policies automate transitions/expirations — a very frequent exam cost-optimization pattern.

front: Amazon S3 Glacier (S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval / Deep Archive)
back: Lowest-cost long-term archival storage classes within S3.

Hybrid storage connecting on-premises applications to AWS storage via standard protocols: File Gateway (NFS/SMB backed by S3), Volume Gateway (iSCSI block volumes, cached or stored mode, backed by EBS snapshots in S3), and Tape Gateway (virtual tape library backed by S3/Glacier, for replacing physical backup tape infrastructure). Exam trigger: 'on-premises application needs to use AWS storage without being rewritten' via a familiar protocol.
A Layer 2 device that forwards frames between devices on the same network segment based on MAC address, giving each connected device its own collision domain (unlike a hub).
A Layer 3 device that forwards packets between different networks/subnets based on destination IP address, using a routing table.

A Layer 1 device that simply repeats/broadcasts any incoming signal out to every other connected port, with no addressing intelligence — all connected devices share one collision domain, causing collisions and poor scalability. Long obsolete, replaced by switches.

A Layer 2 device that connects two network segments and forwards frames between them selectively based on MAC address, reducing collision domains — historically the predecessor to modern multi-port switches.

A general term for any device or service that connects two different networks, often translating between protocols and potentially operating at any OSI layer up to Layer 7 — unlike a router (which just forwards within compatible network types), a gateway can bridge fundamentally different network models.

The set of rules (routes) that determines where network traffic from a subnet or gateway is directed, each route pairing a destination CIDR with a target (local, an Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPC Peering connection, Transit Gateway attachment, VPN, etc.). Every route table always has an unremovable local route covering the VPC's full CIDR, enabling all subnets within the VPC to reach each other by default.

Notation for expressing an IP address range as a base address plus a prefix length (e.g. 10.0.0.0/16), where a smaller prefix number means a larger address range (/16 = 65,536 addresses, /24 = 256 addresses). A VPC's CIDR block can range from /16 to /28, and every subnet's CIDR must fall entirely within its VPC's CIDR and must not overlap any sibling subnet's CIDR.
A segmented range of IP addresses within a VPC's CIDR block, and — unlike in traditional on-premises networking — every AWS subnet is tied to exactly one Availability Zone.

A horizontally scaled, redundant, highly available VPC component that enables bidirectional internet connectivity — one is attached per VPC (a 1:1 relationship), and it performs the 1:1 NAT translating each instance's private IP to its assigned public/Elastic IP for internet-bound traffic. AWS manages it as fully redundant with no bandwidth to provision, so it's never the answer to a scaling or HA question.

front: EMR vs. Athena/Redshift — when do you pick EMR?
back: Pick EMR specifically when you need custom big-data frameworks or existing Hadoop/Spark code — Athena and Redshift are managed-SQL alternatives that don't require bringing your own processing framework.

front: What is the AWS Glue Data Catalog?
back: A central metastore shared by Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR — it's what makes data sitting in S3 queryable as tables across multiple analytics services, without loading it into any of them.

front: Kinesis Data Firehose vs. Kinesis Data Streams — how do you choose?
back: Choose Data Streams when you need custom consumer logic and sub-second processing latency. Choose Firehose when you just need to land streaming data into a destination with no consumer code to write or manage.

front: AppSync vs. API Gateway — how do you choose?
back: Choose AppSync specifically when the requirement is GraphQL, real-time subscriptions, or offline-first mobile sync. Choose API Gateway for REST/HTTP/WebSocket APIs generally.

front: EventBridge vs. SNS — what's the key difference?
back: EventBridge supports content-based filtering across many-to-many event sources/targets and native SaaS integrations. SNS is a simpler pub/sub fan-out mechanism without that content-based routing sophistication.

front: SQS Standard vs. FIFO queues — what's the throughput/ordering tradeoff?
back: Standard queues offer near-unlimited throughput with at-least-once, best-effort ordering. FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and strict ordering, but cap throughput at 300 msg/sec (3,000 with batching/high-throughput mode).

front: SES vs. Pinpoint — what's the difference?
back: SES is the raw email-sending transport, for application-generated transactional/bulk email. Pinpoint adds multi-channel marketing campaign orchestration, targeting, and analytics on top.

front: AWS Budgets vs. Cost Explorer — what's the difference?
back: Budgets is forward-looking: it alerts you when a defined threshold is crossed. Cost Explorer is retrospective: it's for interactively analyzing historical cost/usage trends after the fact.

front: Savings Plans vs. Reserved Instances — what's the key difference?
back: Savings Plans are usage-commitment based (a $/hour spend commitment) and flexible across instance types/services. Reserved Instances are capacity-reservation based and can offer capacity guarantees (zonal RIs) that Savings Plans don't.

front: What are the EC2 purchasing options?
back: On-Demand (no commitment, highest cost), Reserved/Savings Plans (1-3yr commitment for a discount), Spot (up to 90% off, reclaimable with a 2-minute warning — ideal for fault-tolerant/stateless workloads), and Dedicated Hosts/Instances (physical isolation, for licensing or compliance needs).

front: When do you pick Fargate vs. EC2-backed ECS/EKS?
back: Pick Fargate when a question emphasizes 'no server/cluster management' for containers. Pick EC2-backed when you need GPU instances, specific instance types, or tighter cost control at high, steady scale.

front: When do you choose Lambda vs. Fargate/EC2?
back: Choose Lambda for short, event-driven work. Move to Fargate/EC2 when a workload exceeds Lambda's 15-minute max execution timeout or needs persistent state between invocations.

front: ECR vs. CodeArtifact — what's the difference?
back: ECR stores container images. CodeArtifact stores language/library packages (npm, Maven, pip, etc.) — not container images.

front: When do you choose ECS vs. EKS?
back: Choose ECS for simpler orchestration needs without Kubernetes-specific tooling/portability requirements. Choose EKS when Kubernetes-native APIs, existing K8s manifests, or multi-cloud portability are required.

front: What does Aurora Global Database provide?
back: Extends Aurora across Regions with typically <1s replication lag, for disaster recovery and low-latency global reads — the standard exam scenario for cross-Region read scaling and low-RPO/RTO DR.

front: DynamoDB vs. RDS/Aurora — how do you choose?
back: Choose based on access pattern: DynamoDB for key-value lookups at massive scale with careful partition-key design (to avoid hot partitions); RDS/Aurora when you need complex joins or multi-table transactions.

front: ElastiCache: Redis/Valkey vs. Memcached — how do you choose?
back: Choose Redis/Valkey when you need persistence, complex data structures, or replication/pub-sub. Choose Memcached for simple, horizontally-scalable, multi-threaded caching with no data durability requirement.

front: RDS Multi-AZ vs. read replicas — what's the difference?
back: Multi-AZ gives synchronous standby replication for HA/failover, not read scaling. Read replicas give asynchronous, eventually-consistent copies for offloading read traffic (can be cross-Region, and promoted to standalone in a DR scenario). Aurora extends this with up to 15 read replicas (vs. RDS's 5).

front: Redshift vs. Athena — when do you pick Redshift?
back: Pick Redshift for a persistent, high-concurrency data warehouse handling complex joins/aggregations at scale. Pick Athena for ad hoc, serverless queries over S3 without provisioning a cluster.

front: IoT Device Defender vs. IoT Device Management — what's the difference?
back: Device Defender is security monitoring — auditing configs and detecting abnormal behavior. Device Management is operational fleet management — onboarding, monitoring, and remotely managing devices, including OTA updates.

front: Rekognition vs. SageMaker — when do you use which?
back: Use Rekognition when a pre-built vision model suffices. Use SageMaker when you need a custom-trained model — more broadly, SageMaker is for any custom ML model, while Rekognition/Comprehend/Textract/etc. are pre-trained AI services for common tasks that don't require building your own model.

front: CloudTrail vs. AWS Config — what's the difference?
back: CloudTrail tells you WHO made a change and WHEN (the API call). AWS Config tells you WHAT the resource configuration looked like before/after and whether it's compliant. They're frequently paired — confusing which answers 'who changed this' vs. 'is this compliant' is a common exam trap.

front: Wavelength vs. Local Zones vs. Outposts — what's the difference?
back: Wavelength specifically targets 5G carrier networks for latency-sensitive mobile/edge applications. Local Zones are AWS-owned infrastructure placed closer to users generally. Outposts extends AWS infrastructure into a customer's own on-premises data center.

front: Trusted Advisor vs. Compute Optimizer — what's the difference?
back: Trusted Advisor runs broad best-practice/config checks across many categories. Compute Optimizer is specifically a deep rightsizing recommendation engine driven by resource utilization data.

front: When is a Snow Family device the right answer on the exam?
back: When transferring the data over the network would take weeks or months — a function of data size vs. available bandwidth — a physical Snow device beats network transfer.

front: Direct Connect vs. Site-to-Site VPN — what's the tradeoff?
back: Direct Connect gives a dedicated private link — higher cost and lead time, but consistent performance. Site-to-Site VPN is an encrypted tunnel over the public internet — fast to set up, variable performance. A common pattern: use VPN as a fast-to-provision backup path while Direct Connect is being established.

front: ALB vs. NLB vs. GWLB — how do you choose?
back: Application Load Balancer (Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS, content-based routing, WebSocket support) — choose for path-based routing. Network Load Balancer (Layer 4, ultra-high-performance, static IPs, preserves client IP) — choose when a static IP is needed. Gateway Load Balancer — for inserting third-party virtual appliances (like firewalls) transparently inline.

front: Transit Gateway vs. VPC Peering — why does Transit Gateway scale better?
back: VPC Peering is non-transitive and becomes unmanageable at scale — there's no routing through a peered VPC to reach a third one, and you need a new connection (and route table update) per pair. Transit Gateway scales cleanly instead as a hub-and-spoke model.

front: Cognito User Pools vs. Identity Pools — what's the difference?
back: User Pools = authentication (your own user directory with sign-up/sign-in, MFA, social/SAML/OIDC federation, issuing JWTs — answers 'who is this user'). Identity Pools = authorization to AWS resources (federate identities, including from a User Pool, to temporary, scoped AWS credentials).

front: Detective vs. GuardDuty — what's the difference?
back: GuardDuty detects the threat. Detective helps you investigate and analyze the 'why and how' after a GuardDuty finding surfaces.

front: IAM roles vs. users — which is correct for granting service/cross-account access?
back: Roles — they're assumed temporarily and are the correct way to grant permissions to services or cross-account access. Never use long-lived access keys for this. (Also remember: in policy evaluation, an explicit deny always wins over any allow.)

front: KMS vs. CloudHSM — what's the difference?
back: KMS keys live in a shared, multi-tenant HSM fleet managed by AWS — simpler, integrates broadly. CloudHSM gives you a dedicated, single-tenant HSM with exclusive control over the key material.

front: Secrets Manager vs. Systems Manager Parameter Store — what's the difference?
back: Secrets Manager provides built-in automatic rotation and is priced per secret. Parameter Store (SecureString tier) also encrypts values via KMS but has no built-in rotation and is generally free/cheaper — 'needs automatic rotation' is the signal to pick Secrets Manager.

front: What does Shield Advanced add over Shield Standard?
back: Layer 7 protection, near-real-time attack visibility, cost protection against scaling charges incurred during an attack, and access to the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT) — the exam trigger for Advanced is needing cost protection guarantees or 24/7 specialized DDoS support.

front: WAF vs. Shield vs. Network Firewall — what layer does each protect?
back: WAF operates on HTTP request content at Layer 7. Shield handles volumetric Layer 3/4 DDoS attacks. Network Firewall operates broadly at the VPC network layer.

front: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval vs. Deep Archive — what's the retrieval tradeoff?
back: Flexible Retrieval: minutes (Expedited) to hours (Standard/Bulk). Deep Archive: cheapest tier, 12-hour standard retrieval — meant for compliance archives rarely if ever accessed.

front: Why can instances in the same VPC subnet always reach each other with no route table entry?
back: Because a VPC subnet's underlying network fabric performs Layer 2 (switch-like) forwarding invisibly between ENIs in the same subnet — no routing needed. Crossing subnets is what requires Layer 3 routing.

front: What is the VPC router, and where does it live?
back: An implicit router every AWS VPC has, sitting at the reserved first IP of every subnet's CIDR — it consults each subnet's route table to send traffic to another subnet, an Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPC Peering connection, VPN, or Transit Gateway attachment. 'Traffic isn't reaching X' troubleshooting starts here.

front: Does AWS have an equivalent to a network hub?
back: No — AWS has no hub-like construct at all. It's worth knowing purely as OSI-layer contrast to a switch (Layer 2, MAC-aware, separate collision domains per port), reinforcing why VPC subnet-level traffic isolation works the way it does.

front: What's the closest AWS analogy to a network bridge?
back: AWS has no literal 'bridge' service, but VPC Peering and Transit Gateway serve a conceptually similar role at Layer 3 — joining two otherwise-separate networks (VPCs) so traffic can pass between them, even though they operate one layer up from a classic Layer 2 bridge.

front: AWS overloads the term 'Gateway' — what does each one actually do?
back: Internet Gateway (VPC-to-internet), NAT Gateway (outbound-only internet access for private subnets), Virtual Private Gateway (VPN endpoint on the AWS side), Transit Gateway (hub connecting many VPCs/on-premises networks), Direct Connect Gateway (attaches a DX connection to VPCs across regions/accounts), Storage Gateway (on-premises-to-S3/EBS bridge), and API Gateway (client-to-backend API front door). Never assume 'Gateway' implies specific behavior — match the exact service name to the problem.

front: What makes a VPC subnet 'public' vs. 'private'?
back: Purely which route table it's associated with (every subnet has exactly one at a time — the VPC's main route table by default). Public: has a route to an Internet Gateway. Private: doesn't (and typically routes outbound traffic through a NAT Gateway instead).

front: Why does an AWS /24 subnet only have 251 usable IP addresses, not 256?
back: AWS reserves the first 4 and the last 1 IP address in every subnet (5 total) for internal use — network address, VPC router, DNS, future use, and the broadcast address (even though VPC doesn't support broadcast). Separately: overlapping CIDRs between VPCs is the single most common blocker to VPC Peering or Transit Gateway connectivity, since overlapping ranges make routing ambiguous.

front: Why do highly available VPC architectures deploy subnets across multiple AZs?
back: Because every AWS subnet is tied to exactly one Availability Zone (unlike traditional on-premises networking) — so HA design requires at least one subnet per AZ, across multiple AZs, not just multiple subnets in one AZ.

front: Does attaching an Internet Gateway alone make a subnet public?
back: No — the subnet's route table also needs a route sending 0.0.0.0/0 (or ::/0 for IPv6) traffic to the IGW, and the instance itself needs a public or Elastic IP assigned. An IGW alone does nothing.

front: IGW vs. NAT Gateway — what's the traffic-direction difference?
back: Internet Gateway allows direct inbound AND outbound traffic for resources with public IPs. NAT Gateway only allows outbound-initiated traffic for private-IP resources — nothing can initiate a connection in.

A direct, 1:1 private network connection between exactly two VPCs, letting resources in either one talk to each other using private IP addresses — routed by adding an entry to each VPC's route table pointing at the peering connection, not through any gateway or single point of failure.

front: Why does VPC Peering become unmanageable at scale?
back: It's non-transitive: if VPC A peers with VPC B, and B peers with C, A still cannot reach C through B — a direct A–C peering connection is required. Connecting N VPCs so every pair can talk therefore needs N×(N-1)/2 separate peering connections, each with its own route table entries to maintain in every VPC involved — a full mesh that grows quadratically and becomes operationally unmanageable past a handful of VPCs. Transit Gateway replaces this with a hub-and-spoke model: one attachment per VPC, no per-pair connections to create or route tables to update as VPCs are added or removed.

A subnet is 'public' purely because its associated route table has a route sending 0.0.0.0/0 (or ::/0) traffic to an Internet Gateway — the resource itself also needs a public or Elastic IP for the internet to actually reach it, both conditions required together. Typically hosts internet-facing resources: ALBs, bastion hosts, and NAT Gateways.

A subnet whose route table has no route to an Internet Gateway, so resources in it have no direct inbound or outbound path to the internet. Outbound-only internet access (e.g. software updates, calling external APIs) is typically provided via a NAT Gateway placed in a public subnet. Hosts backend resources like databases and internal app servers that should never be directly internet-reachable.

The rules that determine where traffic from a subnet is directed, each pairing a destination CIDR with a target (local, Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPC Peering, Transit Gateway, VPN, etc.). Every subnet is associated with exactly one route table at a time, and that association — not anything inherent to the subnet — is what makes it public or private.

Horizontally scaled, redundant VPC component enabling internet connectivity; exactly one attached per VPC. Performs 1:1 NAT between an instance's private IP and its public/Elastic IP for internet-bound traffic. Attaching it alone does nothing — the subnet's route table must also route 0.0.0.0/0 to it, and the instance needs a public IP assigned.

Managed service placed in a public subnet that lets resources in a private subnet initiate outbound internet connections without being directly reachable from the internet — outbound-initiated traffic only, unlike an Internet Gateway which also allows direct inbound. Highly available within a single AZ only; for resilience, deploy one NAT Gateway per AZ rather than one for the whole VPC, since routing every AZ through a single NAT Gateway creates a cross-AZ dependency (and cross-AZ data transfer cost) if that AZ fails.

A stateful, instance-level (ENI-level) virtual firewall — supports allow rules only (implicit deny for everything else) and automatically allows return traffic for any connection matching an allowed rule, with no separate rule needed for the response. Multiple security groups can attach to one ENI; their rules are combined (most permissive wins).

A stateless, subnet-level virtual firewall — supports both explicit allow AND explicit deny rules, evaluated in numbered order (lowest number first, first match wins). Being stateless, return traffic must be explicitly allowed by a separate rule, unlike security groups which auto-allow it. Every subnet is associated with a NACL (the default NACL allows all traffic); useful for explicitly blocking a specific known-bad IP at the subnet level, which security groups can't do since they only support allow rules.

front: CloudTrail vs. CloudWatch — what's the difference?
back: CloudTrail records API-level activity — every console, CLI, and SDK call made against your account, answering 'who did what, and when' for auditing and security investigation. CloudWatch is operational monitoring — it collects metrics (CPU, latency, custom app metrics), aggregates logs, and triggers alarms that can drive Auto Scaling or notifications, answering 'how is my system performing right now.' They're complementary, not competing: CloudTrail can even deliver its event log into CloudWatch Logs, letting you alarm on specific API activity (e.g. a root login) using CloudWatch's alerting on top of CloudTrail's audit data.

front: SCPs vs. IAM permissions — what's the difference?
back: SCPs (used with AWS Organizations OUs) set the maximum permissions available to every principal in an account, including the root user — an SCP never grants anything itself; you still need an actual IAM policy to perform any action. IAM policies operate at the principal level (users, groups, roles) and grant the real permissions. SCP takes precedence: if a Principal is allowed an action via IAM policy but an attached SCP denies it, the SCP wins — even an Administrator IAM policy can't override an SCP-level Deny.

front: How are SCPs attached, and how does permission inheritance work?
back: SCPs attach to the organization root, an OU, or an individual account, and each attachment either whitelists (Allow) or blacklists (Deny) AWS services/actions. Access to any service not explicitly allowed by the SCPs on an account, its parent OUs, and the management account is denied. An account only has the permissions permitted by every parent above it — a block at any level, implicit (left out of an Allow statement) or explicit (a Deny statement), can't be bypassed by an IAM policy in the account below, even one granting Administrator permissions.

front: Does an SCP restrict the AWS Organizations management account?
back: No — SCPs affect only principals managed by accounts that are members of the organization; the management account itself is not restricted by SCPs applied within that organization.

front: What are the two types of IAM policies?
back: Identity-based policies — attached to an IAM user, group, or role. Resource-based policies — attached to an AWS resource directly, such as an S3 bucket policy. Used together with SCPs, IAM policies give the fine-grained principal/resource-level control that SCPs (organization-wide guardrails) aren't meant to provide.

front: Can an IAM policy restrict the AWS account root user, or attach to an OU?
back: No to both — an IAM policy can only be attached to IAM users, groups, or roles, and can never restrict the root identity of the AWS account. IAM policies also cannot be attached to OUs; only SCPs can. Restricting the root user or applying a rule at the OU level requires an SCP, not an IAM policy.

front: IAM policy evaluation: explicit allow vs. implicit deny vs. explicit deny?
back: Everything is an implicit deny by default. An explicit Allow in a policy overrides that implicit deny. But an explicit Deny always overrides an explicit Allow — no matter how many policies grant an action, a single explicit Deny anywhere in the evaluation blocks it.
